title: ‘Hamilton’ Is a Movie. Or Is It? Plus: Remembering Ennio Morricone.
format: A tribute to Ennio Morricone, a run through the movie news, and then Juliette Lippman joins to argue over whether the filmed Hamilton counts as a movie.

films.title: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly
films.note: The Sergio Leone Man With No Name western whose theme, along with 'The Ecstasy of Gold,' became the shorthand everyone reaches for. Sean notes it has been parodied to the point of absurdity, from Mel Brooks to the Zucker-Abrahams-Zucker movies, and now just lives as music.

films.title: A Fistful of Dollars
films.note: First of the Leone trilogy Morricone is best known for.

films.title: For a Few Dollars More
films.note: The middle chapter of the Dollars trilogy.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in the West
films.note: Sean's favorite thing Morricone ever did, and occasionally his favorite film ever made. The score gives Charles Bronson's character a harmonica that pays off in the final duel.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in America
films.note: The other Leone epic Morricone scored.

films.title: The Battle of Algiers
films.note: Gillo Pontecorvo's film, cited as proof of Morricone's range beyond the westerns.

films.title: Days of Heaven
films.note: Terrence Malick; Sean's example of the elegant, agrarian orchestral side of Morricone.

films.title: The Thing
films.note: John Carpenter usually scores his own films, but for this Howard Hawks remake he brought in Morricone, who wrote what Sean calls basically a Carpenter score, only better. 'An amazing flex.'

films.title: The Untouchables
films.note: Brian De Palma; another late-career collaboration with a filmmaker who had acquired the clout to hire him.

films.title: The Hateful Eight
films.note: Quentin Tarantino, a longtime Morricone champion who had repurposed his old scores for years before finally commissioning one.

films.title: The Mission
films.note: Cited alongside the Malick work as the elegant, beautiful register Morricone could hit.

films.title: Two Mules for Sister Sara
films.note: A post-Man-With-No-Name Clint Eastwood film Sean spent the night listening to; one of the many scores he says are worth hunting for on YouTube.

segments.heading: The news
segments.note: a Swedish shoot, Tenet, and the Academy's new members

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: Ruben Ostlund started shooting his new film in Sweden, where production could actually happen; Sean calls the title one of the greatest he's heard. Woody Harrelson stars, apparently as some kind of naval commander on a ship. Ostlund's The Square and Force Majeure (remade this year as Downhill) came up too.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: Pushed from July 31 to an August 12 release neither host believes will hold (that's the week Amanda booked vacation). A jumping-off point for Christopher Nolan's Leo-coded insistence on the theatrical experience, and the debunked 'no chairs on set' rumor.

segments.heading: ‘Hamilton’ Is a Movie. Or Is It?

films.title: Hamilton
films.note: Lin-Manuel Miranda's musical, filmed across three June 2016 performances and directed by Thomas Kail, arriving on Disney+ for the July 4th weekend after COVID moved its release up 15 months. The trio weighs it as a movie: Amanda and Juliette on the jarring adjustment from a live ensemble to a camera choosing your point of view, Sean on how much livelier it is than the usual static stage capture. Then they get into the criticism five years on: the show's cavalier handling of slavery, its 'great white man' framing, and Miranda's borrowing from hip-hop, against the joy it still delivers. Favorite bits: the 'Satisfied' staging, Renée Elise Goldsberry and Phillipa Soo, and the ending, 'Who Lives, Who Dies, Who Tells Your Story.'
